      • Andrew Wei, CPA, CBV reposted this

        Arlene Dickinson

        • 2nd

        #TeamCanada 🇨🇦Managing General Partner at District Ventures Capital

        Visit my website

        1yr • Edited •

        One of the things I have learned as part of what’s going on—and frankly more than I ever really wanted to—is how the U.S. government actually operates. I used to assume democracy was hard at work, elections, votes, constitutions etc. I believed there were real checks and balances in place to stop undemocratic, dictator-like moves by narcissistic, power-hungry leaders. I sure hope I’m not wrong that the rule of law in the U.S. will prevail. As I’ve tried to make sense of what’s happening, I thought maybe that sharing how I understand it might help others understand it more clearly too. I’m not a lawyer or an economist and so I’m sure over simplified but here’s what I understand Trump is actually doing with these tariffs and why it’s such a dangerous abuse of power. Trump is imposing tariffs on Canada, Mexico, and China under a law meant for national security emergencies. This is a complete misuse of executive authority. The US law he’s invoking, the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA), was never created to be used this way. It was designed for extreme situations—like freezing assets linked to terrorism—sanctions against bad actors (you know like, um, Russia for example) not for slapping tariffs on allies and trade partners. By twisting it to fit his agenda and claiming a national emergency at the border, he’s setting a dangerous precedent. If he gets away with this, he can declare an “emergency” anytime he wants, disrupting global trade and world order with no congressional oversight. And that? That’s dangerous. I’ve said it a lot but these tariffs arent about national security for the U.S. He’s using an emergency powers law—one designed for sanctions against hostile nations—to bypass Congress and impose economic penalties on America’s closest allies. This is about power, plain and simple and was never about the border. Trump is using the presidency to manipulate global markets, punish allies, and avoid the checks and balances that are supposed to prevent exactly this kind of overreach. And with his grip on right-wing media and social platforms, Americans aren’t getting the full story. At some point, the courts will have to step in because this kind of abuse can’t go unchallenged. Weaponizing trade, harming innocent civilians, and sidling up to war criminals are not just a president trying to protect America. They are the markings of a dictatorship or autocratic empire being built that needs to be toppled before it’s too late. I hope sharing this is helpful to explaining some of what’s going on. Trumps abuse of power is going to impact Americans and Canadians. But how we as Canada move forward and deciding what leader we will choose to battle him one on one is one of the most important decisions we will ever have to make. Elbows up Canada. Elbows up! ❤️🇨🇦

      • Andrew Wei, CPA, CBV reposted this

        Arlene Dickinson

        • 2nd

        #TeamCanada 🇨🇦Managing General Partner at District Ventures Capital

        Visit my website

        1yr •

        I just finished a US-Canada Trade Council meeting. As promised, I’m sharing what I know real time —and I’ll keep updating you as more information comes in. By now, you’ve all heard that Trump is moving ahead with 25% tariffs on 🇨🇦 , effective at midnight. 🇨🇦 will respond in kind—we are not backing down. $155 billion in counter-tariffs on US goods will be implemented, starting at midnight, with full implementation over the next 21 days. There will be support programs announced for businesses affected by these tariffs. You are not in this alone. Entrepreneurs & biz builders will do what we’ve always done—find a way to turn this challenge into opportunity and into strength. You all know this started with Trump’s border concerns—yet Canada has done what we said we would - and more - to address those concerns. EG: *Fentanyl trafficking from Canada into the U.S. has been reduced to nearly zero. This is a proven fact. *We have invested over $1 billion in border security to help tackle both drug trafficking and immigration concerns. *We have done our part But we all know it was never really about that. It was about controlling our resources minerals, geography and economy So unfortunately here we are. Canada is ready. The road ahead won’t be easy, but we are climbing it together. It takes two to negotiate—but it also takes a leader who isn’t trying to bully their way into power by hurting businesses and families on both sides of the border. This isn’t just bad policy; it’s shameful. We will not kneel down to someone who backs war criminals before working with democratic allies and neighbors. Many of you ask what you can do now; 🇨🇦Stay strong, Canada. Elbows up for what we’re fighting for, and hearts out for each other. 🇨🇦Support Canadian businesses. If there was ever a time to buy Canadian, it’s now. Choose Canadian goods and services like never before. 🇨🇦Push for real change. Interprovincial trade barriers have finally started coming down, and we’re making progress thanks to cooperation with provinces. But we must go further—especially when it comes to recognizing qualifications for job movement between provinces.Right now, it’s easier for an engineer in Quebec to have their credentials recognized in France than in Ontario! That’s not just frustrating—it’s holding Canada back. 🇨🇦Skilled professionals should be able to work anywhere in Canada without unnecessary red tape. If you’re qualified in one province, you should be qualified in all provinces. Lobby your premiers. Demand full recognition of professional qualifications across the country. The more we remove these barriers, the stronger and more competitive Canada becomes. 🇨🇦Stick together. Remember what we have. We are rich in resources and skilled people. We need to all be solution-oriented, support those with less, and keep building the Canada we believe in. I hope this update helps. We’ve stood up before, and we will again. The only way forward is together.❤️🇨🇦

      • Andrew Wei, CPA, CBV reposted this

        Colin Deacon 🇨🇦🇺🇦

        • 2nd

        Independent Senator, Senate of Canada

        1yr •

        Ottawa has become a city where there seems to be absolutely no respect for how hard it is to run a successful business. Consider the past few years… the challenges of COVID, supply chain disruptions, the resulting inflation, and the (more than) doubling of borrowing costs. It’s been really tough.

        In the past few weeks, two new problems have been thrust upon small businesses… timed to cause the greatest harm, a         Canada Post / Postes Canada strike that is crippling small businesses... and then the Prime Minister and his Deputy Prime Minister suddenly spring a supposed Christmas “tax holiday” on Canadians, surprising their Cabinet and Caucus, federal departments, the Provinces and the businesses who have been conscripted to deliver their “present”. It’s as if this all part of some sort of evil plan to test the resilience of small business during their most crucial sales season.

        The random list of items selected, the challenge of reconfiguring POS systems (twice) to try to align with that unexplainable list, then having to explain the lack of a tax holiday on one but not another item, defend your decisions to the CRA, have this happen in two separate tax years, pay the (up to 15%) difference when you get it wrong… it’s insane.

        Then, in stark contrast to the realities that every business MUST deal with (and can only avoid at their peril), Canada Post and its union continue to demonstrate their complete inability to adjust to market realities like grownups. As a result, they have forced yet another devastating market reality on others.

        Honestly, with Purolator and UPS now pausing services, it may not matter if the parties find some sort of agreement, even as soon as this weekend — the backlog may be too great, and too many small businesses customers may already cancelled their orders and moved on (Amazon may be the only winner in this strike).

        Additionally, I have absolutely no faith that a settlement will bring the essential changes needed to make Canada Post competitive and sustainable. Given the $3 billion in losses since I became a senator, and three quarters of a billion in losses last year, Canada Post is unsustainable. At this stage, I believe that it is best to let it go bankrupt and force a complete rethink of is operations. Regardless, it would be tragic if the can were to be simply be kicked down the road, yet again.

        Canada Post is an essential service in so many rural and remote communities, but any thought of subsidizing it in its current form would be a huge error. It’s place in our economy must be reimagined within the context of much more competitive and productive alternatives. Unfortunately, consideration of market realities does not seem to be on the table.

        Please, if you can, prioritize shopping in person at small businesses for as long as you are able. Things are really rough and they represent the heart of most neighbourhoods and communities.

        The Globe and Mail

        75,515 followers

        1yr •

        In the latest edition of The Globe and Mail's Business Cycle newsletter, we’re talking about what the Canada Post strike means for people who still receive cheque payments, the "vibecession" in Canada and Couche-Tard’s persistent takeover bid of Japan’s Seven & i.

        Give the newsletter a read below and check out the business pieces from Rob Csernyik, John Rapley and Andrew Willis.

        Hats off! We commend all 128 candidates who successfully passed the latest CBV Membership Qualification Examination (MQE). No small feat.       Experience

      Manager, Corporate Finance & Treasury

      Enercare Inc.

      May 2024 - Present · 1 yr 11 mos

      Enercare is a portfolio company of Brookfield Infrastructure Partners Reported directly to the SVP of Finance: Lead strategic finance initiatives across pricing strategy, liquidity planning, and capital markets reporting for a large Canadian energy services platform. Built unit-economics pricing models correcting legacy cost allocation and improving shareholder IRR reporting accuracy by ~4%. Manage long-range liquidity forecasting and capital allocation models optimizing utilization of a $3B+ securitization platform. Developed full three-statement financial models for banking partners supporting the underwriting of a $250MM subsidiary securitization facility. Serve as the primary finance partner to product and commercial teams on new product economics, pricing strategy, and partnership evaluations. Lead preparation of board and shareholder materials, including quarterly performance updates and financial modelling for the company’s 5-year strategic plan. Recruited and oversaw a dedicated data analyst focused on automation of existing manual processes and improving overall reporting efficiency and accuracy.

      financial modelling, Structured Finance and +2 skills

      Private Debt Investment Analyst

      Third Eye Capital

      May 2023 - May 2024 · 1 yr 1 mo

      Toronto, Ontario, Canada

      At Third Eye Capital, I played a pivotal role in identifying and analyzing private debt investment opportunities, focusing on businesses in transition. My responsibilities included conducting in-depth financial assessments and collaborating with management teams to optimize operational strategies. This approach not only supported our clients' growth but also enhanced our investment portfolio's performance. Deal Experience: Go-private (MBO) of a Canadian based technology company with revenue of ~$400MM

      Due Diligence, Investment Analysis and +4 skills
